title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series Ep 29: How to Replace Backgrounds with AI
description:
In this episode, learn how to use ComfyUI to remove and replace backgrounds on product images, portraits, or pets! This step-by-step guide covers everything from masking techniques to generating creative backgrounds using AI and prompts.

What’s Included:

- Removing complex or white backgrounds.
- Setting proper dimensions for best results.
- Creating and inverting alpha masks for precise edits.
- Combining AI tools like Control Net and inpainting for seamless background swaps.
- Fixing imperfections with Photoshop.
- Enhancing images with detailed prompts and upscalers.

Other Episodes to Watch: Episode 14, 19, 22

Get the workflows and instructions from discord
https://discord.gg/gggpkVgBf3

Check Other Episodes
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL-pohOSaL8P9kLZP8tQ1K1QWdZEgwiBM0

Unlock exclusive perks by joining our channel:
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCmMbwA-s3GZDKVzGZ-kPwaQ/join

#replacebackground #comfyui #aitutorial
transcript: Welcome to Episode 29 of our Comfy UI tutorial series.  Today I will show you how to replace the background of your product images.  All you need is an image of your product, but this method also works for people or animals.  You simply need a main subject, and the background will be removed and replaced with a different  image that you prompt. I will open the first workflow, which you can get for free from Discord,  just like all the workflows from my episodes. I'll start with this one called  Replace Complex Background because it works best for images with a background that isn't a  simple solid color. The workflow is split into two parts. In the first part, we load the image  and remove the background. Click on Upload and add your own image. I'll use this perfume bottle as  an example. What's important is to set the right dimensions here. It works best with values around 1024  pixels. Right now, it's a square ratio. I resized it so it doesn't get too large in the case  sampler. If you want a 16-9 ratio, you can use something like 1024 by 576 pixels, for example.  Once the image is resized, I remove the background. More about that in Episode 22.  A mask is also created, which I'll use to remove the background. Right now, the mask is for the  object, so I'm using an invert mask node to invert the mask, so we only select the background.  What you see in white is the selection and what is black is not selected. I remember it like this.  Think of it as light. If there's light, you can see things. If there's no light, it's all black,  and you can't see anything. Then, we enable the second group, which is actually in painting from  Episode 19, combined with control net from Episode 14. There's one extra step. Right click on this  alpha image and select copy image. You could load the image and select the mask from the output  folder, but I prefer just selecting the node. You'll see an outline when it's selected, and using  the shortcut control plus V to paste it. Now we have our mask with the background selection.  Next, we need a prompt. Remember, flux works well with long descriptive prompts. When I run the  workflow, it will use the flux model, and it will use the in-paint nodes that I explained in Episode 19,  but with control net as well. Right now, it uses the base image for in-painting because it needs  some background to in-paint something. The size of the image that we set and resize is also the size  of the final image, and it's the size used for in-painting because it's the entire background.  So the final image will look like this. Try to keep it around 1024 pixels or multiples of 8 or 64  for width and height. For control net, I use the alpha mask to have a clear depth map of the subject.  You can play around with values like denoys or apply control net, but I've been able to get all  kinds of replacements with these values, and here's the result. It's not perfect every time it  depends on the settings, the prompt, and how well the alpha mask cuts out the subject.  But it does a good job of creating a believable background, making it look like the subject is  actually there, with the floor beneath it, and sometimes even reflections. If you don't like it,  or if you want another version, just run the workflow again. Look at how the lights and shadows  interact. We have the shadow of the bottle, and it looks like it belongs there.  Here's how I like to create prompts that integrate my product. First, I upload my original image,  in this case, the perfume bottle. Then, I ask chat gpt to create a stable diffusion prompt for the  bottle, but I want it to be placed on a rock with moss somewhere in the jungle, with warm lights  during golden hour, and some red flowers blurred in the background. The prompt I gave could stand  alone, but I prefer more detailed prompts, which is why I use chat gpt. I guide it by explaining  how I want the scene to start, with the subject, the environment, what's around it, and how I want  the light to be. Think like a photographer. If you were taking a photo of your product, how would  it look? How would you create and arrange the scene? Once I have the long prompt, I copy it and  paste it into the workflow. After running the workflow, I get this result. It has the rock with moss,  the red blurred flowers, and the warm light. Now, I want to make some changes. On the first try, I get this. The reflection of the bottle is now visible. I might need to run another  seed to get better water, or I could have asked for water ripples. I enable the second group,  copy and paste the burger mask, and then I want to create a good prompt for this. I use  ChatGPT to generate something interesting with fire behind it. It gives me a nice detailed prompt,  so I paste it into the workflow and test it. Here's the result. It has a little edge from behind  the burger, but it's not bad for the first try. At this point, the depth map only considers the burger.  Not the background, so I could create any background. However, if you want to pick information  from the background as well, you can change the image going to the pre-processor. Instead of  using just the mask, you can use the image coming from that composite node, which contains both the  burger and the background. I can connect that, and of course, you can try different pre-processors  like canny, depth, or whatever fits, like we did in episode 14. Now, the model can take some  information from the background as well, and you can see in the generated result that we got the  brick wall behind the burger. If you want to match the background even more, you can reduce the  denoise value until you get a closer match to the original background. Sometimes, using upscalers  can help you get better quality. For example, if I open the upscaler workflow, I can use the perfume  bottle image, which has a few jagged edges on the top and sides to see if I can fix those and  get a larger image. I'll add a few words about what the subject is, and when I run it, I get  three different versions saved, one upscaled about two times, another upscaled about four times,  and the last one resides back to 10, 24 pixels to provide a smaller, sharper version. You can use  higher denoise values like 0.95 if you want more freedom, but small details such as text might be  replaced with other things. So, experiment to see what works best for you. Let's compare. If I  slide to the left, it's before, and when I move to the right, you can see the after result.  It added some extra details and made it a little sharper. I wanted to keep the text, so that's why  I used a denoise value of around 0.8 or 0.85, but you can try higher values if you don't have text,  and you might get even better results. I hope this helps. I wanted to show you an example with a  person. Take a look at this woman, while the cutout is pretty good, there's still a slight grayish,  bluish tint around the edges. Now, we could place her on a background with a similar color to match,  but let's say I want her on a purple background. This is how it looks. You can see that the colors  don't quite match because the mask is a bit outside the hair contour, and what's inside the mask  is kept. So, what can we do in this case? What worked for me was going to the blur mask and increasing  the value. For example, I tried with 16, and now when I run the workflow, you can see that because  the mask is blurred, the edges contract a little. In this case, I don't want the mask to be inverted. I want the selection to be on the subject,  so I can change it. To do that, right click on invert mask and choose bypass. This will bypass  the node and continue with the rest of the process. Now, when I run the workflow, you can see that the  woman is selected because she's in white. Now, I can copy the new mask, enable the group,  and paste the new mask there. Let's change the prompt to something simple, like a portrait of a  woman with blonde hair. Here's the result. Notice how the woman changes, but the background stays  the same. Let me drag the original background here, so you can see it better. As you can see, the  original background is preserved, and only the woman has been changed. If you want a more subtle  change, you can play with the denoise value between 0.8 and 1. You can get subtle changes to the  character. So, with basic image to image, if you decrease the denoise value, the result will be  more similar to the original, but if you increase the denoise, the changes will be more pronounced.  One last thing to keep in mind is that the image ratio you use should match the width and height,  but again, try to keep it around 10, 24 pixels, and multiples of 8 or 64 for better results.  For example, if I change the height to 768 and run the workflow, you'll see that it gets distorted,  so for a square image, use the same width and height. If I load a landscape image,  you can see that in square ratio, it's distorted. If I reduce it to 768, it's still distorted,  but at 576 pixels, it looks just right.
